note_data.title: Theory of the firm
keyPoints: **Short run** is the time period where at least one factor of production is fixed (usually capital), while **long run** is when all factors are variable
**Profit maximisation** occurs where marginal cost (MC) equals marginal revenue (MR), as any other output level would reduce total profit
In **perfect competition**, firms are price takers facing a perfectly elastic demand curve at the market price
A **monopolist** is a price maker who can set price above marginal cost, creating deadweight loss and allocative inefficiency
**Oligopoly** features few large firms with interdependent pricing decisions, often leading to non-price competition and kinked demand curves
**Monopolistic competition** combines many firms with differentiated products, allowing some price-making power in the short run
**Economic profit** is total revenue minus total costs (including opportunity costs), while **normal profit** occurs when economic profit is zero
**Barriers to entry** prevent new firms entering a market, maintaining supernormal profits for existing firms in the long run
**Price discrimination** allows firms to charge different prices for the same product to different consumers, increasing producer surplus

definitions.definition: The additional cost of producing one more unit of output
The additional revenue gained from selling one more unit of output
A market structure with many buyers and sellers, homogeneous products, perfect information, and freedom of entry and exit
A market structure where there is only one seller of a product with no close substitutes and high barriers to entry
Profit above the normal profit required to keep factors of production in their current use

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Break down the cause and effect relationship with detailed explanation
Consider both advantages and disadvantages, then reach a supported judgment

modelAnswer.question: Explain why a profit-maximising firm will produce where marginal cost equals marginal revenue. [6 marks]
modelAnswer.answer: A profit-maximising firm produces where MC = MR because this is the output level that generates maximum total profit. If MC < MR, the firm should increase output because the additional revenue from selling one more unit exceeds the additional cost of producing it, thus adding to total profit. If MC > MR, the firm should reduce output because the cost of producing the last unit exceeds the revenue it generates, reducing total profit. Only at MC = MR is profit maximised, as any deviation from this point would reduce total profit. At this equilibrium, the firm cannot increase profit by changing output levels. This principle applies to all profit-maximising firms regardless of market structure.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: If MC < MR, increase output for more profit
Mark 3: If MC > MR, reduce output to avoid losses
Mark 4: Only at MC = MR is profit maximised
Mark 5: No incentive to change output at equilibrium
Mark 6: Applies to all market structures

commonMistakes: Never confuse normal profit with supernormal profit — normal profit is the minimum required to stay in business
Students often forget that in perfect competition, firms are price takers, not price makers
Don't say monopolists always make profit — they can make losses if average cost exceeds price
Never confuse short run and long run — it's about factor fixity, not time periods
Students incorrectly think all oligopolies collude — many compete vigorously through non-price competition
examinerTips: Market structure questions appear in almost every Paper 2 exam
Always draw labelled cost and revenue diagrams for analysis questions
Use Pakistani examples: PSO for monopoly, banking sector for oligopoly
Remember to distinguish between economic and accounting profit
Efficiency analysis always scores well — mention allocative and productive efficiency
formulas: MR = Change in TR / Change in Q
MC = Change in TC / Change in Q
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Cost and revenue diagram showing MC, MR, AC, and AR curves with profit-maximising output at MC = MR intersection, plus shaded areas for profit/loss
